story: February 20 is Love Your Pet Day, when pet owners everywhere go extra crazy showing their furry, feathered, or scaly friends just how much they care! title: A Journey to Ancient Angkor
story: Zhou Daguan, a brave Chinese explorer, set off on an incredible adventure in 1296. He left his hometown of Mingzhou to visit Angkor, the magnificent capital of the Khmer Empire. Zhou became the first outsider to write about daily life in this mysterious city. His journey began on February 20, opening a window into a world few had ever seen before.
activity_question: Who was Zhou Daguan?
follow_up_question: What made Zhou Daguan's journey special?

title: Lone Survivor of Icy Shipwreck
story: Disaster struck at sea in 1856. The John Rutledge, a steam packet-ship, crashed into a massive iceberg while sailing from Liverpool to New York on February 20. As icy waters flooded in, the ship sank rapidly. Out of many souls aboard, only one person survived the terrifying ordeal - Thomas Nye from New Bedford. This event reminds us how dangerous ocean travel could be, even with steam power.
activity_question: What type of obstacle did the John Rutledge hit?
follow_up_question: Why might icebergs be especially dangerous for ships?

title: Sailing to the Edge of Antarctica
story: Icy winds whipped across the deck as Captain James Weddell pushed his ship further south than anyone before. In 1823, this brave English explorer reached an astonishing 74°15' South latitude on February 20. That's closer to the South Pole than any human had ever sailed! Weddell's daring voyage helped map the mysterious frozen continent of Antarctica.
activity_question: What did Captain James Weddell do on February 20, 1823?
activity_choices: Saw penguins for the first time
follow_up_question: Why do you think exploring Antarctica was so challenging in the 1800s?

title: MIT Builds a New Path for Architects
story: Blueprints and dreams took shape as the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) made history in 1865. On February 20, they opened the first collegiate architectural school in the United States. This groundbreaking program laid the foundation for future architects to learn their craft. Students could now study the art and science of designing buildings right on campus.
activity_question: What did MIT open in 1865?
activity_choices: The first US collegiate architectural school
activity_answer: The first US collegiate architectural school
follow_up_question: Why do you think it's important for architects to study at a college?

title: A Palace of Art Opens Its Doors
story: Imagine a giant treasure chest filled with beautiful paintings and ancient artifacts. That's what visitors found when the Metropolitan Museum of Art opened in New York City in 1872. On February 20, art lovers stepped into a world of wonder, with rooms full of masterpieces from around the globe. The museum started small but grew to become one of the world's largest art collections.
activity_question: What opened in New York City in 1872?
follow_up_question: What kinds of things might you see in an art museum?

title: Pedaling to Victory: Roland's Cycling Triumph
story: Wheels spinning and heart pounding, Roland Liboton raced to glory on February 20, 1983. The Belgian cyclist conquered muddy trails and steep hills to become the world champion in cross-country cycling. His incredible skill and determination pushed him to the top of the podium, inspiring cyclists everywhere to chase their dreams.
activity_question: What type of cycling did Roland Liboton become world champion in?
follow_up_question: What country was Roland Liboton from?

title: Japan's Eye in the Sky
story: Zoom! On February 20, 1983, Japan sent a special satellite named Tenma soaring into space. This high-tech explorer had an important job: to study powerful x-rays coming from distant stars and galaxies. Tenma circled Earth, peering deep into the cosmos to help scientists unravel the mysteries of our universe.
activity_question: What was Tenma's job in space?
bonus_fact: Tenma means 'Pegasus' in Japanese, a flying horse from mythology.

title: Racing History Made at Daytona 500
story: Engines roared as Cale Yarborough sped to victory in the 1977 Daytona 500. This thrilling race on February 20 marked his second win in the Great American Race. But another driver made headlines too. Janet Guthrie broke barriers as the first woman to compete in NASCAR's top series, finishing an impressive twelfth place.
activity_question: Who was the first woman to compete in NASCAR's top series?
follow_up_question: What place did Janet Guthrie finish in the race?

title: Columbia's Engines Roar to Life
story: Flames and smoke billowed as Columbia's powerful engines roared to life in 1981. This wasn't a launch, but a crucial test. On February 20, NASA fired up the space shuttle's main engines for 20 seconds while it stayed firmly on the ground. This 'flight readiness firing' helped ensure everything was perfect before Columbia's historic first mission.
activity_question: What was the purpose of firing Columbia's engines on the ground?
follow_up_question: Why do you think it's important to test spacecraft before launching them?

title: A Young King's Coronation
story: Trumpets blared as nine-year-old Edward became King of England in 1547. On February 20, the young prince was crowned King Edward VI after his father, the famous Henry VIII, passed away. Despite his age, Edward took on the huge responsibility of ruling a country. He was England's first monarch raised as a Protestant.
activity_question: How old was Edward when he became king?
follow_up_question: What major change did Edward bring to England's religion?
